artifacts.content:
Hi Em,

Kids are finally in bed, Eric’s grading labs at the dining table, and I’m hiding in the bedroom with the laptop, so it feels like a good time to actually write more than three lines.

I keep thinking about Mom and Dad and Laguna Ridge. Ever since Dad’s partial disability in 2021 and those back‑to‑back rent hikes at the Elk Grove apartment, it’s like there’s this timer ticking in my head. That’s a big reason I’m pushing so hard on the Riverbend Station Mixed-Use Transit Village. We carved out about 62 income‑restricted units with a 55‑year affordability covenant, and the idea of them being able to live near the Riverbend Light Rail Station and the American River Greenway without another surprise rent spike is what keeps me going on the bad days.

It’s all tangled up with the kids too. Remember that near‑miss with Sophie back on October 7, 2019? The distracted driver turning into the old warehouse driveway at Harbor and East Riverbend almost sideswiped her in the crosswalk by Riverbend Elementary. I filed that Safe Routes to School complaint with Principal Karen Alvarez and the Riverbend Police incident report was #19-8742. That whole mess is why I fought so hard for fewer curb cuts and the big 1.8‑acre central plaza in the project.

Day‑to‑day life is the usual chaos: Sophie’s robotics club is building a “future city” model (she keeps stealing my trace paper), Caleb’s soccer practices on Tuesdays and Thursdays, and Max has decided 5:45 a.m. is the ideal time for walkies. We’re still doing Sunday runs to Elk Grove every other week for Mom’s groceries and meds.

Anyway, I just wanted you to know there’s a straight line from our family’s stuff to the decisions I’m making at work. Call me this weekend if you’re up for a long catch‑up.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why Riverbend Station feels so personal

artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I wanted to follow up on our conversation about workload and project phasing now that the Riverbend Station Mixed-Use Transit Village is moving from entitlement into implementation.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Spring 2022 and my three weeks of FMLA medical leave for anxiety and burnout when I was juggling overlapping EIRs. You might remember our April 4, 2022 email thread where I looped in Dr. Patel at Midtown Behavioral Health Clinic about how the late nights and constant deadlines were affecting sleep and panic symptoms. I really don’t want to end up back in that place, and I also don’t want junior staff going through something similar.

With that in mind, I’m proposing we keep the Riverbend Station demolition and construction on a phased schedule over roughly 36–42 months, with site prep and warehouse demolition starting in mid‑2027 instead of trying to compress everything into a two‑year window. Besides being better for staff capacity, it should help us coordinate with Public Works and RUSD on some of the site‑specific issues we’ve already seen.

For example, in March 2023 Eric’s physics classroom at Riverbend High had to be evacuated for two days (March 14–15) because of a hazardous materials odor drifting from the existing auto body shop at 1820 Harbor Boulevard. That incident is in the Riverbend Unified School District log, and it’s why we’re baking in conditions of approval for fully enclosed loading areas, upgraded ventilation, and a prohibition on new auto‑related uses in the PD.

Happy to discuss more in person, but I wanted this context captured in writing as we finalize the internal staffing plan.

Best,
Laura
Planning Manager, Community Development

metadata.subject: Riverbend Station phasing, staff capacity, and conditions of approval

artifacts.content:
Personal planning note – Riverbend Station + family context

1. Harbor/East Riverbend safety narrative
- Remember Sophie’s near-miss on 10/7/2019 at the Harbor Blvd/East Riverbend Pkwy crosswalk by Riverbend Elementary.
- Distracted driver turning into the old warehouse driveway at the future Riverbend Station site; filed Safe Routes to School complaint with Principal Karen Alvarez and RPD incident report #19-8742.
- That experience is why I pushed so hard for the 1.8-acre central plaza, reduced curb cuts, and better pedestrian priority at the intersection.

2. Trail connection + CPTED
- On 5/22/2022, evening bike ride with Caleb along the Riverbend Greenway segment behind the old warehouse at 4301 East Riverbend Parkway.
- Man exposing himself; reported to Riverbend Police as incident #22-3117.
- Use this as a reminder to insist on CPTED features in the multi-use trail connection: continuous lighting, clear sightlines (no hidden corners by landscaping), emergency call boxes, and regular patrol coordination.

3. Parents + affordability
- Mom (Grace) and Dad (David) at Laguna Ridge Apartments in Elk Grove; Dad’s partial disability in 2021 and reduced engineering income triggered rent stress.
- Riverbend Station’s ~62 income-restricted units with a 55-year affordability covenant (Regulatory Agreement anticipated late 2028) are exactly the kind of housing they and other low-/very low-income seniors need near the Riverbend Light Rail Station and American River Greenway.

4. Self-care + phasing
- Don’t forget Spring 2022 FMLA leave (three weeks) for anxiety/burnout after overlapping EIRs. Talked with Dr. Patel at Midtown Behavioral Health about realistic timelines.
- Keep demolition/construction phasing (36–42 months, starting mid-2027) as a non-negotiable for staff sanity.

Also: buy more sticky notes and printer paper; Sophie keeps “borrowing” them for robotics sketches.

metadata.title: Riverbend Station – personal context + design priorities

artifacts.content:
Hey Maya,

Thanks again for grabbing coffee last week. It helped more than you know to talk to someone who gets both the planner brain and the mom brain.

I’ve been mulling over the whole “why am I doing this to myself” question with Riverbend Station. On paper it’s just another 18.4-acre mixed-use infill project with an EIR, a General Plan Amendment from Light Industrial to Transit-Oriented Mixed Use, zoning to MU-TO, a PD, Design Review, Vesting Tentative Map, all the usual alphabet soup. But emotionally it’s so tied up with our family.

There’s Sophie almost getting sideswiped back in 2019 at Harbor and East Riverbend when that driver turned into the old warehouse driveway by Riverbend Elementary. Filing the Safe Routes to School complaint with Principal Alvarez and the RPD incident #19-8742 was the first time I really felt like “if I don’t fix this, who will?”

Then there was the indecent exposure incident with Caleb on the Riverbend Greenway behind 4301 East Riverbend Parkway in May 2022 (RPD #22-3117). That’s why I’m borderline obsessive about the trail’s CPTED details—lighting, call boxes, no blind corners.

Layer on top of that my parents at Laguna Ridge in Elk Grove, dealing with rent hikes after Dad’s partial disability in 2021. When I’m arguing for the 15% affordable units (about 62) with the 55-year covenant, I’m literally picturing them riding the Riverbend Light Rail to doctor’s appointments instead of white-knuckling it on Highway 99.

Meanwhile, regular life: Sophie’s robotics, Caleb’s soccer, Max shedding on everything, and my mom’s cardiology follow-ups. Eric’s already warning me that once construction starts in 2027, I’ll be living on Harbor Boulevard.

Anyway, thanks for listening to all my urbanist therapy. Let’s plan another walk around Civic Center Plaza soon.

– Laura

metadata.subject: Why Riverbend Station feels like my third child

artifacts.content:
Event: Therapy – Midtown Behavioral Health Clinic
Date: Thursday, April 7, 2022
Time: 4:00–5:00 p.m.
Location: Midtown Behavioral Health Clinic, Sacramento
Attendees: Laura Chen; Dr. Patel

Notes:
- Follow-up after three-week FMLA medical leave from City of Riverbend Community Development Department (March 2022) for anxiety and burnout related to overlapping EIRs.
- Review coping strategies for returning to full workload: boundaries on evening email, realistic project timelines, delegation to junior planners.
- Talk through physical symptoms that cropped up before leave (racing heart at night, waking up at 3 a.m. thinking about traffic modeling for Harbor Blvd, snapping at Eric and the kids).
- Discuss Riverbend Station Mixed-Use Transit Village specifically: how to phase demolition of existing warehouses starting mid-2027 and stretch construction over approx. 36–42 months so I’m not recreating the same overload conditions. Bring up idea of documenting this rationale in email to HR Manager Elena Rodriguez.
- Also want to process Eric’s March 14–15, 2023 classroom evacuation at Riverbend High due to hazardous materials odor from the auto body shop at 1820 Harbor Blvd (RUSD incident log). Even though that’s “work,” it’s also our family. Make sure I’m not internalizing responsibility for every bad thing that happens near Harbor/East Riverbend.
- Personal: check in about ongoing worry over Mom’s early-stage heart failure and Dad’s partial disability and rent stress at Laguna Ridge Apartments.

Reminder: leave Civic Center Plaza by 3:15 p.m., Eric to handle kids’ pickup and Max’s walk.

Laura: Hey, just checking in about your walk tonight. If you’re planning to take the kids down to the Riverbend Greenway, maybe avoid the segment behind the old warehouse at 4301 East Riverbend Parkway after dark.

Neighbor (Jess): Oh? Something happen?

Laura: Yeah, back on May 22, 2022, Caleb and I were biking there around 7:30 p.m. and a guy exposed himself. We ended up filing a report with Riverbend Police (incident #22-3117). They responded quickly, but it really shook Caleb. Since then I’m a lot more cautious about that stretch in the evening.

Neighbor (Jess): Yikes. I had no idea. Thanks for telling me.

Laura: No problem. The upside is that because of that and Sophie’s near-miss at Harbor & East Riverbend in 2019, I’ve been able to push for better design in the Riverbend Station project—more lighting and emergency call boxes on the trail, fewer driveways near the crosswalks by Riverbend Elementary.

Neighbor (Jess): I’m glad you’re in charge of that stuff. We just want to get the kids’ energy out before bedtime.

Laura: Totally get it. We usually do the section closer to the American River and loop back by Willow Trace Way when it’s late. Max doesn’t care where we walk as long as he can sniff everything.

Neighbor (Jess): 😂 Same with our dog. Thanks again. Let’s do a joint kid-bike ride this weekend when it’s light out.

Laura: Sounds good. Saturday morning after Caleb’s soccer maybe? I’ll text you Friday to confirm.

Hi Mom,

Just wanted to send a longer update since it’s hard to talk about everything when we’re rushing around on Sundays.

First, I’ve been thinking a lot about you and Dad at Laguna Ridge. Ever since Dad’s partial disability in 2021 and the rent increases, I know it’s been stressful. One of the reasons I’ve been pushing so hard on the Riverbend Station Mixed-Use Transit Village is to make sure we get those approximately 62 income-restricted units with the 55-year affordability covenant. The idea is that low- and very low-income seniors can live closer to the Riverbend Light Rail Station and the American River Greenway without worrying about big rent spikes.

I can’t promise anything specific for you two, but it helps me to think that families like ours might have more options in a few years, especially once the Regulatory Agreement is recorded (they’re aiming for late 2028).

Construction is still a ways off—site preparation and demolition of the old warehouses probably won’t start until mid-2027, and then it’ll take 36–42 months. I actually argued for that longer schedule after I had to take three weeks of FMLA leave in Spring 2022 when I got burned out from managing too many EIRs at once. My therapist, Dr. Patel at Midtown Behavioral Health Clinic, helped me see that I can’t take care of you, the kids, and my job if I run myself into the ground.

Kids are okay—Sophie’s robotics club is building a tiny “transit village” model (she keeps asking me about plazas), and Caleb scored a goal at soccer this week. Eric says hi and reminds you he still owes you a batch of his miso salmon.

Love you,
Laura
